html:
VII

Olivier and Armand

In the meantime Olivier, deeply disappointed at not having found his Uncle Edouard, and unable to bear his solitude, turned his thoughts towards Armand with a heart aching for friendship. He made his way to the Pension Vedel.

Armand received him in his bedroom. It was a small, narrow room, reached by the backstairs. Its window looked on to an inner courtyard, on to which the water-closets and kitchens of the next-door house opened also. The light came from a corrugated zinc reflector, which caught it from above and cast it down, pallid, leaden and dreary. The room was badly ventilated; an unpleasant odour pervaded it.

вАЬBut one gets accustomed to it,вАЭ said Armand. вАЬMy parents, you understand, keep the best rooms for the boarders who pay best. ItвАЩs only natural. I have given up the room I had last year to a VicomteвБ†вАФthe brother of your illustrious friend Passavant. A princely roomвБ†вАФbut under the observation of RachelвАЩs. There are heaps of rooms here, but not all of them are independent. For instance, poor Sarah, who came back from England this morning, is obliged to pass, either through our parentsвАЩ room (which doesnвАЩt suit her at all) to get to her new abode, or else through mine, which, truth to tell, is really nothing but a dressing-room or box-room. At any rate, I have the advantage here of being able to go out and in as I please, without being spied upon by anyone. I prefer that to the attics, where the servants live. To tell the truth, I rather like being uncomfortably lodged; my father would call it the вАШlove of maceration,вАЩ and would explain that what is hurtful to the body leads to the salvation of the soul. For that matter, he has never been inside the place. He has other things to do, you understand, than worrying over his sonвАЩs habitat. My papaвАЩs a wonderful fellow. He has by heart a number of consoling phrases for the principal events of life. ItвАЩs magnificent to hear him. A pity he never has any time for a little chat.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ YouвАЩre looking at my picture gallery; one can enjoy it better in the morning. That is a colour print by a pupil of Paolo UcelliвАЩsвБ†вАФfor the use of veterinaries. In an admirable attempt at synthesis, the artist has concentrated on a single horse all the ills by means of which Providence chastens the equine soul; you observe the spirituality of the look.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ That is a symbolical picture of the ages of life from the cradle to the grave. As a drawing, not much can be said for it; its chief value lies in its intention. Further on you will note with admiration the photograph of one of TitianвАЩs courtesans, which I have put over my bed in order to give myself libidinous thoughts. That is the door into SarahвАЩs room.вАЭ

The almost sordid aspect of the place made a melancholy impression on Olivier; the bed was not made and the basin on the washstand was not emptied.

вАЬYes, I fix up my room myself,вАЭ said Armand, in response to his anxious look. вАЬHere, you see, is my writing table. You have no idea how the atmosphere of the room inspires me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАШLвАЩatmosph√®re dвАЩun cher r√©duit.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЩ I even owe it the idea of my last poemвБ†вАФThe Nocturnal Vase.вАЭ

Olivier had come to see Armand with the intention of speaking about his review and asking him to contribute to it; he no longer dared to. But ArmandвАЩs own conversation was coming round to the subject.

вАЬThe Nocturnal VaseвБ†вАФeh? What a magnificent title!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ With this motto from Baudelaire:

вАШFunereal vase, what tears awaitest thou?вАЩ6

вАФвБ†Baudelaire

вАЬI take up once more the ancient (and ever young) comparison of the potter creator, who fashions every human being as a vase destined to holdвБ†вАФah! what? And I compare myself in a lyrical outburst to the above-mentioned vaseвБ†вАФan idea which, as I was telling you, came to me as the natural result of breathing the odour of this chamber. I am particularly pleased with the opening line:

вАШWhoeвАЩer at forty boasts no hemorrhoids.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЩ

I had first of all written, in order to reassure the reader, вАШWhoeвАЩer at fiftyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЩ but I should have missed the assonance. As for вАШhemorrhoids,вАЩ it is undoubtedly the finest word in the French languageвБ†вАФindependently of its meaning,вАЭ he added with a saturnine laugh.

Olivier, a pain at his heart, kept silent. Armand went on:

вАЬNeedless to say, the night vase is particularly flattered when it receives a visit from a pot filled with aromatics like yourself.вАЭ

вАЬAnd havenвАЩt you written anything but that?вАЭ asked Olivier at last, desperately.

вАЬI was going to offer my Nocturnal Vase to your great and glorious review, but from the tone in which you have just said вАШthat,вАЩ I see there isnвАЩt much likelihood of its pleasing you. In such cases the poet always has the resource of arguing: вАШI donвАЩt write to please,вАЩ and of persuading himself that he has brought forth a masterpiece. But I cannot conceal from you that I consider my poem execrably bad. For that matter, I have so far only written the first line. And when I say вАШwritten,вАЩ itвАЩs a figure of speech, for I have this very moment composed it in your honour.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No, really? were you thinking of publishing something of mine? You actually desired my collaboration? You judged me, then, not incapable of writing something decent? Can you have discerned on my pale brow the revealing stigmata of genius? I know the light here is not very favourable for looking at oneself in the glass, but whenвБ†вАФlike another NarcissusвБ†вАФI gaze at my reflection, I can see nothing but the features of a failure. After all, perhaps itвАЩs an effect of chiaroscuro.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No, my dear Olivier, no; I have done nothing this summer, and if you are counting on me for your review, you may go to blazes. But thatвАЩs enough about me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Did all go well in Corsica? Did you enjoy your trip? Did it do you good? Did you rest after your labours? Did you.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Olivier could bear it no longer:

вАЬOh! do shut up, old boy. Stop playing the ass. If you imagine I think itвАЩs funny.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬAnd what about me?вАЭ cried Armand. вАЬNo, my dear fellow, no; all the same IвАЩm not so stupid as all that. IвАЩve still intelligence enough to understand that everything IвАЩve been saying is idiotic.вАЭ

вАЬCanвАЩt you ever talk seriously?вАЭ

вАЬVery well; weвАЩll talk seriously, since seriousness is the style you favour. Rachel, my eldest sister, is going blind. Her sight has been getting very bad lately. For the last two years, she hasnвАЩt been able to read without glasses. I thought at first it would be all right if she were to change them. But it wasnвАЩt. At my request, she went to see an oculist. It seems the sensitiveness of the retina is failing. You understand there are two very different thingsвБ†вАФon the one hand, a defective power of accommodation of the crystalline, which can be remedied by glasses. But even after they have brought the visual image to the proper focus, that image may make an insufficient impression on the retina and be only dimly transmitted to the brain. Do I make myself clear? You hardly know Rachel, so donвАЩt imagine that I am trying to arouse your pity for her. Then why am I telling you all this?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Because, reflecting on my own case, I became aware that not only images but ideas may strike the brain with more or less clearness. A person with a dull mind receives only confused perceptions; but for that very reason he cannot realize clearly that he is dull. He would only begin to suffer from his stupidity if he were conscious of it; and in order to be conscious of it, he would have to become intelligent. Now imagine for a moment such a monsterвБ†вАФan imbecile who is intelligent enough to understand that he is stupid.вАЭ

вАЬWhy, he would cease to be an imbecile.вАЭ

вАЬNo, my dear fellow; you may believe me, because as a matter of fact, I am that very imbecile.вАЭ

Olivier shrugged his shoulders. Armand went on:

вАЬA real imbecile has no consciousness of any idea beyond his own. I am conscious of the beyond. But all the same IвАЩm an imbecile, because I know that I shall never be able to attain that вАШbeyondвАЩ!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬBut, old fellow,вАЭ said Olivier, in a burst of sympathy, вАЬwe are all made so that we might be better, and I think the greatest intelligence is precisely the one that suffers most from its own limitations.вАЭ

Armand shook off the hand that Olivier had placed affectionately on his arm.

вАЬOthers,вАЭ said he, вАЬhave the feeling of what they possess; I have only the feeling of what I lack. Lack of money, lack of strength, lack of intelligence, lack of loveвБ†вАФan everlasting deficit. I shall never be anything but below the mark.вАЭ

He went up to the toilette table, dipped a hairbrush in the dirty water in the basin and plastered his hair down in hideous fashion over his forehead.

вАЬI told you I hadnвАЩt written anything; but a few days ago, I did have an idea for an essay, which I should have called: вАШOn Incapacity.вАЩ But of course I was incapable of writing it. I should have said.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But IвАЩm boring you.вАЭ

вАЬNo; go on; you bore me when you make jokes; youвАЩre interesting me very much now.вАЭ

вАЬI should have tried to find throughout nature the dividing line, below which nothing exists. An example will show you what I mean. The newspapers the other day had an account of a workman who was electrocuted. He was handling some live wires carelessly; the voltage was not very high; but it seems his body was in a state of perspiration. His death is attributed to the layer of humidity which enabled the current to envelop his body. If his body had been drier, the accident wouldnвАЩt have taken place. But now letвАЩs imagine the perspiration added drop by drop.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ One more dropвБ†вАФthere you are!вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt understand,вАЭ said Olivier.

вАЬBecause my example is badly chosen. I always choose my examples badly. HereвАЩs another: Six shipwrecked persons are picked up in a boat. They have been adrift for ten days in the storm. Three are dead; two are saved. The sixth is expiring. It was hoped he might be restored to life; but his organism had reached the extreme limit.вАЭ

вАЬYes, I understand,вАЭ said Olivier. вАЬAn hour sooner and he might have been saved.вАЭ

вАЬAn hour! How you go it! I am calculating the extremest point. It is possible. It is still possible.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It is no longer possible! My mind walks along that narrow ridge. That dividing line between existence and nonexistence is the one I keep trying to trace everywhere. The limit of resistance toвБ†вАФwell, for instance, to what my father would call temptation. One holds out; the cord on which the devil pulls is stretched to breaking.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ A tiny bit more, the cord snapsвБ†вАФone is damned. Do you understand now? A tiny bit lessвБ†вАФnonexistence. God would not have created the world. Nothing would have been. вАШThe face of the world would have been changed,вАЩ says Pascal. But itвАЩs not enough for me to thinkвБ†вАФвАШif CleopatraвАЩs nose had been shorter.вАЩ I insist. I ask: shorter, by how much? For it might have been a tiny bit shorter, mightnвАЩt it?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Gradation; gradation; and then a sudden leap.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Natura non fecit saltus. What absurd rubbish! As for me, I am like the Arab in the desert who is dying of thirst. I am at that precise point, you see, when a drop of water might still save himвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ or a tear.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

His voice trailed away; there had come into it a note of pathos which surprised Olivier and disturbed him. He went on more gentlyвБ†вАФalmost tenderly:

вАЬYou remember: вАШI shed that very tear for thee.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЩвАКвАЭ

Olivier remembered PascalвАЩs words; he was even a little put out that his friend had not quoted them exactly. He could not refrain from correcting: вАЬвАКвАШI shed that very drop of blood for thee.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЩвАКвАЭ

ArmandвАЩs emotion dropped at once. He shrugged his shoulders:

вАЬWhat can we do? There are some who get through with more than enough and to spare.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Do you understand now what it is to feel that one is always вАШon the border lineвАЩ? As for me, I shall always have one mark too little.вАЭ

He had begun to laugh again. Olivier thought that it was for fear of crying. He would have liked to speak in his turn, to tell Armand how much his words had moved him, and how he felt all the sickness of heart that lay beneath his exasperating irony. But the time for his rendezvous with Passavant was pressing him; he pulled out his watch.

вАЬI must go now. Are you free this evening?вАЭ

вАЬWhat for?вАЭ

вАЬTo come and meet me at the Taverne du Panth√©on. The Argonauts are giving a dinner. You might look in afterwards. ThereвАЩll be a lot of fellows thereвБ†вАФsome of them more or less well knownвБ†вАФand most of them rather drunk. Bernard Profitendieu has promised to come. It might be funny.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm not shaved,вАЭ said Armand a little crossly. вАЬAnd then what should I do among a lot of celebrities? But, I sayвБ†вАФwhy donвАЩt you ask Sarah? She got back from England this very morning. IвАЩm sure it would amuse her. Shall I invite her from you? Bernard could take her.вАЭ

вАЬAll right, old chap,вАЭ said Olivier.
